sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Ligament sprains range from mild stretch injuries to high-grade instability. The clinical priority is recognising unstable or associated injuries and restoring function without unnecessary immobilisation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Joint pain after twist, roll, pivot, or impact
- Swelling, bruising, tenderness over ligament structures
- Feeling of instability or giving way
- Reduced range and reluctance to load the joint

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider fracture, dislocation, tendon rupture, syndesmosis injury, or osteochondral injury if pain is severe, swelling is rapid, or function is markedly limited
- High-grade sprains may need bracing, imaging, or orthopaedic input
- Recurrent sprains often reflect poor proprioception, load management, or unresolved instability

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen deformity, weight-bearing, bony tenderness, and neurovascular status
- Use protection, compression, and activity modification within scope
- Escalate when the patient cannot load safely, has major swelling, or may have a fracture or unstable joint injury

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Grade likely severity and begin staged loading, range restoration, and balance retraining as tolerated
- Use brace or taping advice when it improves safe function in early recovery
- Address recurrence prevention through strength, proprioception, footwear, and work-task modification
- Refer for imaging or specialist assessment if instability, locking, or high-grade injury is suspected

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The ability to walk does not exclude a clinically significant sprain
